title: Astronauts Fly Far from Earth and See the Moon Up Close
summary: Four astronauts on a NASA mission called Artemis II are coming back to Earth after a big adventure. They flew around the Moon and went further away from Earth than any people have ever gone before. While they were behind the Moon, they lost contact with Earth for 40 minutes, but then they could talk again. They saw a total eclipse of the Sun when the Moon blocked its light. The astronauts took pictures and drew sketches of the Moon's surface, especially the far side that we never see from Earth. They broke a record set by Apollo 13 in 1970. The President called to congratulate them, and they asked to name some craters on the Moon. It was an exciting trip to explore space.

keywords.explanation: the big round object that orbits Earth
the best or farthest someone has done something
when one object in space blocks the light of another
the part of the Moon we cannot see from Earth

What did the astronauts do when they lost contact with Earth?
What did the astronauts see when the Moon blocked the Sun's light?
Which mission held the previous record for farthest travel from Earth?
What did the astronauts use to record what they saw on the Moon?
Why is the far side of the Moon special?
options: It is always visible from Earth
It is never visible from Earth
questions.correct_answer: It is never visible from Earth

background_read: Space exploration is when people send rockets and astronauts to learn about space. NASA is the American space agency that runs missions like Artemis II. The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, and astronauts have visited it before, like in the Apollo missions. An eclipse happens when the Moon moves between Earth and the Sun, blocking sunlight. The far side of the Moon is mysterious because it always faces away from Earth, so we only see it from space.
Article_Structure: WHO: Four astronauts from NASA's Artemis II mission
WHAT: They flew around the Moon, broke a distance record, and recorded the Moon's surface
WHERE: In space, around the Moon and far from Earth
WHY: To explore space and set new records for human travel

perspectives.description: The mission shows how humans are pushing boundaries to learn more about space and the Moon.
